title: Synergistic improvement of specific strength and plasticity achieved in Ti-based metallic glass designed based on quasicrystal structure
authors: Zhengqing Cai, Zijing Li, Shidong Feng, Limin Wang, Riping Liu
abstract: Achieving a balance between low density, high strength, and good ductility remains a major challenge in the development of structural materials. Ti-based bulk metallic glasses (BMGs) have attracted considerable attention due to their exceptionally high specific strength. However, the intrinsic strength-plasticity trade-off has hindered their practical applications. Based on a quasicrystal-derived structural heredity and minor-element microalloying, this work realizes a synergistic enhancement of specific strength and plasticity in Ti-based BMGs. The resulting ((Ti_{40}Zr_{40}Ni_{20})_{72}Be_{28})_{97}Al_{3} BMGs demonstrate an ultrahigh specific strength of 5.34 \times 10^5 \text{ N}\cdot\text{m}\cdot\text{kg}^{-1}, establishing a new record for Ti-based BMGs, along with a plastic strain of 13\%, breaking through the traditional strength-plasticity limitation of BMGs. Structural analyses show that Al microalloying effectively inherits and modulates the short-range order derived from quasicrystalline structures, thereby achieving an observed synergistic enhancement in both strength and plasticity. This work provides new insights into composition design and lightweight structural applications of Ti-based BMGs.
summary: This paper demonstrates a method to simultaneously increase the strength and ductility of Ti-based metallic glasses by adding small amounts of Aluminum. This is achieved by modulating the short-range order of the glass, resulting in a new record for specific strength in this class of materials.
main_problem: The intrinsic strength-plasticity trade-off in Ti-based bulk metallic glasses (BMGs) prevents their use in high-performance structural applications.
main_result: By microalloying with 3 at.% Al, the researchers achieved a record-breaking specific strength of 5.34 x 10^5 N*m/kg and a plastic strain of 13%, breaking the traditional strength-plasticity limitation.
method: Arc melting and copper mold suction casting were used for synthesis, followed by XRD, DSC, Archimedes density measurements, and uniaxial compression testing.
model_or_system: A series of Ti-based bulk metallic glasses with the chemical formula ((Ti40Zr40Ni20)72Be28)100-xAlx, designed based on a quasicrystal-derived structural heredity.
key_observables: Glass transition temperature (Tg), crystallization temperature (Tx), supercooled liquid region width (delta Tx), yield strength, plastic strain, and specific strength.
important_parameters: Aluminum atomic percentage (x ranging from 1.5 to 7.5 at.%), density, and atomic size mismatch.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows XRD patterns and DSC curves for structural verification; Figure 2 presents uniaxial compression stress-strain curves; Figure 3 displays SEM images of fracture surfaces and shear bands; Figure 4 compares density, specific strength, and plasticity across various alloy systems.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the strength-plasticity trade-off, describes the synthesis and characterization of Al-microalloyed Ti-based BMGs, analyzes the thermal and mechanical properties, proposes a deformation mechanism based on structural heterogeneity, and concludes with future directions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
